artifact.name: The Sumerian Planisphere
artifact.observationDate: c. 3300 BCE (controversial - based on precession calculations)
artifact.dateNote: The physical tablet dates to ~700 BCE from Ashurbanipal's library. The '5,500 years old' claim refers to a disputed theory that it records observations from ~3300 BCE, calculated by matching star positions to ancient skies using precession.
artifact.description: A circular clay tablet divided into 8 sectors by lines radiating from the center, containing observations of the night sky including constellations, stars, and possibly a meteor impact event.

sectors.name: Pisces / Fish
sectors.interpretation: Western fish of Pisces constellation, marking the celestial equator in ancient times.

sectors.name: Aries / Hired Man
sectors.interpretation: The Hired Man or Agrarian Worker, associated with agricultural calendar and spring equinox.

sectors.name: Pleiades / Stars
sectors.content: Seven dots representing the star cluster
sectors.interpretation: The Seven Stars (MUL.MUL literally 'Stars Stars'), crucial for agricultural timing and navigation.

sectors.name: Orion / True Shepherd
sectors.interpretation: The True Shepherd of Anu, one of the most prominent constellations, associated with the god Ninurta.

sectors.name: Gemini / Great Twins
sectors.interpretation: The Great Twins, associated with the gods Lugalgirra and Meslamtaea.

sectors.name: Leo / Lion
sectors.interpretation: The Great Lion, associated with the summer heat and the goddess Ishtar in her warlike aspect.

sectors.name: Triangulum / Plough
sectors.interpretation: The Plough constellation, namesake of the MUL.APIN astronomical compendium.

sectors.name: Pegasus / Field
sectors.interpretation: The Field, representing the Great Square of Pegasus, used as a reference for the first point of Aries.

centerSymbol.name: Celestial Axis / Ecliptic Pole
centerSymbol.description: The central circle may represent the celestial pole or the ecliptic, serving as the axis around which the heavens appear to rotate.

astronomical.title: Star Chart Interpretation
astronomical.description: The tablet represents the night sky as observed from Mesopotamia, divided into eight equal sectors corresponding to specific constellations and celestial regions.

kofelImpact.title: Astrophysical Event Theory
kofelImpact.description: Bond & Hempsell (2008) proposed that the tablet records a catastrophic asteroid impact at Kofels, Austria around 3123 BCE, with the sectors tracking the object's trajectory.

calendaric.title: Agricultural Calendar
calendaric.description: The sectors may correspond to agricultural seasons and important stellar risings/settings used for planting and harvest timing.

culturalContext.significance: This planisphere represents one of the earliest systematic attempts to map and understand the night sky, demonstrating sophisticated observational astronomy in ancient Mesopotamia.
